label: ai
topic: A woman's day told in clock-ticks — discovering her husband has died
text:
8:07 a.m.

You back the car out of the driveway and feel the bump. A small bump, barely perceptible. You stop. You get out. The squirrel is under the rear left tyre, its body flattened, its eye open. You stand there for longer than makes sense, looking at the eye, and then you get back in the car and drive your daughter to school.

8:34 a.m.

The drop-off line at Westfield Elementary moves at the speed of continental drift. Your daughter is talking about a girl named Sienna who told everyone her family has a pool, but it is an above-ground pool, which apparently matters. You say "mmhmm" at the right intervals. Your daughter gets out without saying goodbye. She is seven. Goodbyes are not yet something she understands as necessary.

9:15 a.m.

The house is quiet in the way houses are quiet when they have recently been full. You start cleaning. You wipe the kitchen counters. You pick up the socks your husband left next to the hamper, not inside it, a habit you have discussed perhaps four hundred times across twelve years. You put the socks in the hamper. Your hands know the motion.

10:42 a.m.

You call Michael about dinner. It rings four times and goes to voicemail. His message is too long. He recorded it three years ago and includes an unnecessary instruction to leave a message after the beep. You do not leave a message.

11:30 a.m.

You vacuum the upstairs hallway. The vacuum finds a Lego, a hairband, and a penny. You empty the canister and notice how much of what accumulates in a house is skin cells and fabric fibres. Dust is mostly us, you read somewhere.

12:15 p.m.

You eat lunch standing at the counter. Half a sandwich, an apple. You call Michael again. Voicemail. You text: "Call me when you get a chance."

1:00 p.m.

You fold laundry on the bed. Michael's shirts on the left. Your things on the right. Your daughter's small bright clothes in the middle. There is one sock without a match, grey, partnerless for weeks. You put it back in the drawer anyway.

2:45 p.m.

You pick up your daughter. She is holding a painting of a house with a purple sky. "The sky isn't purple," you say, and immediately wish you hadn't. "It is sometimes," she says. She is right.

3:30 p.m.

Skating practice. You sit in the bleachers watching your daughter move across the ice with the determined gracelessness of someone who has been skating for exactly four months. She falls. She gets up. The cold settles into your bones. You call Michael from the bleachers. Voicemail.

5:15 p.m.

You cook dinner for two. Pasta with the red sauce your daughter will eat, the only sauce she will eat. You set three places out of habit, then clear the third.

6:40 p.m.

Your daughter eats and talks about Sienna's pool again. You check your phone under the table. No missed calls.

7:30 p.m.

Bath time. Story time. Your daughter asks for the one about the bear, which you have read so many times you could recite it in your sleep. She is asleep before the bear finds its way home.

8:15 p.m.

You call Michael. Voicemail. You call his office. No answer. You call his friend Rob, who says he hasn't seen him since Tuesday.

9:47 p.m.

You call the police.

10:12 p.m.

The doorbell rings. You know before you open it. You know from the lateness of the hour, from the shape of the figures visible through the frosted glass. You have been knowing all day, you realise. Since the squirrel. Since the bump under the tyre. Since the morning went quiet in a way that was different from ordinary quiet.

You open the door.
